relevant_cases.verbatim_excerpt: A 10-year-old girl, previously healthy, presented with a 1-day history of diffuse abdominal pain, watery diarrhea, and a temperature of 37.6  degreesC. She reported no rash or pruritus. On initial evaluation at a local clinic, she was hypotensive and tachycardic (heart rate 156 beats/min), with a tendency toward somnolence. Rapid infusion of crystalloid restored her blood pressure to 120/68 mmHg within 15 minutes, but within hours her systolic pressure again dropped to 40 mmHg, prompting transfer to our hospital.
relevant_cases.relevance_notes: Capillary leak syndrome causes sudden refractory hypotension, hemoconcentration, and shock without pericardial effusion - mimics tamponade-like physiology with hemoglobin drop from hemoconcentration and vascular collapse

relevant_cases.verbatim_excerpt: A 26-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of a confluent red macular rash on his face, neck, and upper chest, associated with sore throat, nausea, palpitations, fever, and malaise. He had fever to 38.7  degreesC, tachycardia to 130 bpm with a systolic murmur, and left hypochondrial pain. Examination revealed multiple tender head-and-neck lymph nodes. The rash, which began on the neck, spread to the face and back; it was blanching and mildly pruritic, with less pronounced involvement of the arms, legs, and abdomen.
relevant_cases.relevance_notes: Severe drug hypersensitivity can cause systemic shock with hemodynamic collapse, fever, and hemoglobin drop (drug-induced hemolysis) - mimics post-procedural refractory hypotension

relevant_cases.verbatim_excerpt: A 51-year-old woman with a history of systemic hypertension treated with atenolol presented with a 4-5 day history of progressively worsening headache, neck pain, severe anxiety, chest discomfort, nausea, vomiting, abdominal distension, tachypnea, dry cough, and lightheadedness. She denied fever or urinary symptoms. Laboratory evaluation demonstrated leukocytosis (22,500 leukocytes/mm3) with neutrophilia. She was started on ceftriaxone for suspected meningitis.
relevant_cases.relevance_notes: Pheochromocytoma crisis causes sudden cardiovascular collapse with refractory hypotension and shock from catecholamine storm - can mimic tamponade-like physiology

relevant_cases.verbatim_excerpt: A 54-year-old woman with insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (noncompliant) and recurrent urinary tract infections presented with a 3-day history of right lower-quadrant and right flank dull abdominal pain, fever, and dysuria. She had been lethargic, prompting ED evaluation. She denied medications or allergies, reported a 30-pack-year smoking history, and no alcohol or drug use.
relevant_cases.relevance_notes: Septic shock causes refractory hypotension refractory to fluids and pressors, with hemodynamic collapse - differential for post-procedural shock

relevant_cases.verbatim_excerpt: A 35-year-old Hispanic man presented with 1 week of generalized malaise, abdominal pain, fatigue, subjective fever, sore throat, joint pain, watery diarrhea, oral-intake intolerance, and a petechial skin rash. The rash began as blisters on his palms and soles, progressing to non-confluent erythematous macules with burning and itching that spread to his arms, chest, and trunk.
relevant_cases.relevance_notes: TTP causes microangiopathic hemolysis with hemoglobin drop and can present with acute cardiovascular collapse from systemic microvascular thrombosis

relevant_cases.verbatim_excerpt: A 41-year-old previously healthy Persian man presented with a 7-day history of fever, myalgia, and malaise and a 2-day history of gastrointestinal bleeding manifested as coffee-ground vomitus and melena. Fourteen days before presentation, he had traveled to Karbala, Iraq. He was evaluated twice at other centers for presumed COVID-19 infection because of fever and myalgia but did not improve and had not developed GI bleeding at those times.
relevant_cases.relevance_notes: Viral hemorrhagic fever causes hemoglobin drop from bleeding, systemic shock, and refractory hypotension - systemic cause of shock with hemoglobin decline

relevant_cases.verbatim_excerpt: A 78-year-old man was admitted with a urinary tract infection complicated by toxic metabolic encephalopathy. His medical history included parkinsonism--for which he took levodopa-carbidopa--and three-vessel coronary artery bypass grafting. During the first night of hospitalization, he became agitated and combative and received a total of 30 mg of intramuscular ziprasidone over 12 hours. The following morning, the nurse noted generalized muscular rigidity.
relevant_cases.relevance_notes: NMS causes drug-induced systemic collapse with autonomic instability including labile blood pressure, though typically with hyperthermia and rigidity

relevant_cases.verbatim_excerpt: A 29-year-old male presents to the emergency department with progressive right groin pain and swelling for 24 hours. He reports symptom onset shortly after sexual intercourse. He notes increased tenderness and a persistent bulge in the right groin. He had a normal bowel movement one day before presentation and passed gas on the day of presentation. He denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, fever, chest pain, shortness of breath, dysuria, hematuria, flank pain, penile discharge, testicular pain, or skin lesions.
relevant_cases.relevance_notes: Arterial rupture causes acute hemorrhagic shock with hemoglobin drop and refractory hypotension without pericardial effusion - similar mechanism to access-site bleeding
